data.title: European stocks rise, long-dated yields ease ahead of US jobs data
data.description:
Key points:
  • European stocks rise, after S&P record high
  • Traders wait for U.S. jobs data, focus on rate cut expectations
  • Oil prices in third day of declines

European equities rose in early trading on Friday while long-dated bond yields eased, as expectations for U.S. rate cuts helped markets overcome concerns about fiscal deficits in various countries.

The S&P 500 hit a new all-time high on Thursday after weekly jobs data showed more jobless claims than expected. Asian stocks tracked Wall Street higher overnight.

At 0752 GMT, the MSCI World Equity Index was up 0.3% on the day EURONEXT:IACWI and Europe's STOXX 600 was up 0.4% , set to end the week slightly higher overall after recovering from a dip earlier in the week.

The FTSE 100 was up 0.3% CURRENCYCOM:UK100 and France's CAC 40 was up 0.2% .

Markets are all but certain of a quarter-point cut at the conclusion of the Fed's two-day rate-setting meeting on September 17, according to LSEG data.

Traders will be looking to the monthly U.S. jobs report due later in the session to confirm their expectations. A weaker labour market boosts stocks because it raises expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut rates.

"We have already seen yesterday the sign that perhaps there will be a weakening in jobs, paving the way for a done deal in September," said Francesco Sandrini, head of multi-asset strategies at Amundi.

Today's numbers "can confirm to some extent an easing stance of the Federal Reserve," Sandrini added.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell had already reinforced rate cut speculation with an unexpectedly dovish speech at last month's Fed symposium in Jackson Hole.

"Unless it's an absolutely stellar payrolls print, it's hard to see too much that's going to change the market away from locking in a September cut," said Ken Crompton, head of rates strategy at National Australia Bank.

"Beyond that, the terminal rate and how you get there, that's arguably still up for grabs."

Market sentiment has recovered in recent sessions after global stocks fell earlier this week and long-date bond yields in Europe hit their highest in years, as investors became concerned about the state of various countries' finances, particularly Britain and France.

Yields eased on Friday, with France's 30-year yield at 4.3944%, down from a peak of 4.523% on Wednesday (FR30YR=RR), and the UK's 30-year yield at 5.563%, after borrowing costs hit their highest level since 1998 earlier in the week.

The benchmark 10-year German yield was at 2.7122% (DE10YT=RR). German industrial orders unexpectedly fell in July, data on Friday showed.

Yields on 30-year Treasuries (US30YT=RR) were at 4.8593%, having touched their lowest in three weeks during Asian trading.

The U.S. dollar eased, with the dollar index down 0.2% at 98.054 , while the euro was up 0.2% at $1.1678 .

After months of negotiations, the U.S. signed a deal to impose lower auto tariffs on Japan. The dollar was down 0.3% against the yen, with the pair at 148.14 .

Oil prices were in their third day of declines. Brent crude futures fell 0.5% to $66.65 a barrel, while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude eased 0.6% to $63.05.

Gold was steady at $3,546.24, having hit a record peak of $3,578.50 on Wednesday.

data.title: Resolutions of the extraordinary general meeting of Ørsted A/S
data.description:
Ørsted A/S (Orsted)

Resolutions of the extraordinary general meeting of Ørsted A/S

05-Sep-2025 / 10:37 CET/CEST

5.9.2025 10:36:58 CEST | Ørsted A/S | Decisions of general meeting

Today, Ørsted A/S held an extraordinary general meeting, where the following decisions were adopted:

  • The proposed authorisation for the Board of Directors to increase Ørsted A/S’s share capital with pre-emptive rights for the existing shareholders was approved.
  • The existing authorisation for the Board of Directors to increase Ørsted A/S’s share capital without pre-emptive rights for the existing shareholders as set out in articles 4.2.1 and 4.2.2 of the Articles of Association was repealed.
  • The authorisation to register the resolutions of the general meeting was approved.

About Ørsted

The Ørsted vision is a world that runs entirely on green energy. Ørsted develops, constructs, and operates offshore and onshore wind farms, solar farms, energy storage facilities, and bioenergy plants. Ørsted is recognised on the CDP Climate Change A List as a global leader on climate action and was the first energy company in the world to have its science-based net-zero emissions target validated by the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi). Headquartered in Denmark, Ørsted employs approx. 8,200 people. Ørsted's shares are listed on Nasdaq Copenhagen (Orsted). In 2024, the group's revenue was DKK 71.0 billion (EUR 9.5 billion). data.title: SNG: Ford eyes South Africa people-mover market with new Tourneo Custom models
data.description:
Imran Salie

Now that the dust has settled on the introduction of two new players to the local line-up Ford’s Tourneo Custom van, it’s a good time to have a look at what these two new vans offer and put into context the company’s decision to expand the range.

Ford has filled a gap in the market for prospective buyers looking to stand out from ordinary and not break the bank with the Tourneo Sport and Titanium X. Let me explain, the ordinary yet capable Tourneo Custom Trend is the Plain Jane that does its job at a reasonable price, but lacks the aura and road presence of say a Mercedes-Benz V-Class, but its siblings Sport and Titanium X siblings can stand up to a pricier luxury van from design standpoint at a lower price.

It's the Blue Oval’s attempt to capture some market share while the segment is one of the only categories expected to grow in the coming years, as explained by Ford Motor Company of South Africa’s CEO Neale Hill at the local launch of the Tourneo Custom Sport and Titanium X.

Market share year to date, June 2025, the Ford Tourneo Custom holds 6%, while the segment leader is the futuristically designed Hyundai Staria with 37%. The Mercedes-Benz V-Class follows with 24%. Other players the Ford Tourneo Custom is up against the Toyota Quantum, Volkswagen Caravelle and Kombi, and Mercedes-Benz Vito.

The Tourneo Custom Sport and Titanium X bring some serious upgrades that set them apart from the standard Trend. The Sport is all about dynamic flair, with a honeycomb-style grille, body-colour bumper, assertive rear spoiler, dual black-and-blue stripes, and 17-inch alloy wheels as standard.

Inside, it gets partial leather trim with blue accent stripes, a 13-inch touchscreen with Sync 4, and a suite of driver-assist features including adaptive cruise control and collision mitigation. Optional extras like the Mobile Office package make it practical for work while keeping that sporty, attention-grabbing look.

The Titanium X leans more into luxury, with Matrix LED headlamps, 19-inch multi-spoke wheels, and a classy front grille. Its cabin is a step up in comfort with heated second-row seats, a powered front passenger seat, and an optional near full-length panoramic roof via the Luxe Pack.

Add a 14-speaker B&O sound system, ambient lighting, and a 360-degree camera, and you’ve got a people-mover that blends practicality with high-end style. B

Both models keep the eight-seat layout and share the 2L single-turbo diesel engine, delivering 125kW and 390Nm, paired with an eight-speed automatic transmission and multiple drive modes for whatever urban or freeway adventure comes next.

Conclusion

I had the opportunity to drive both new models in KwaZulu-Natal with Ford SA in mixed driving conditions, such as urban streets and freeway cruising. The in-car feel is carried over from the LWB variant, ensuring that the driver has a similar experience to driving a large SUV from the cabin.

The single-turbocharged four-cylinder diesel engines proved themselves worthy on our drive route, successfully moving driver and passenger from point A to point B with not much fuss. It would, however, be interesting to see how well it does the same job with a full house of eight people seated. Both models sipped between 8 and 9L per 100km on our trip.

Visually, the Tourneo Custom Sport is just cool; the name is definitely befitting. Expect a decent amount of stares and thumbs up from strangers in this variant. The Tourneo Titanium X is less souped up visually, but leans more towards the high-end, classy design side. Both models successfully separate themselves from their established counterparts in the design department.

Overall, the new players are a welcome addition to the local Ford portfolio. They are solid people-movers that combine practicality with visual flair, offering buyers a choice between the Sport at R1 ,218,000 and the Titanium X at R, 264,000, both including Ford’s service plan, warranty, and roadside assistance packages.

data.title: Hang Seng Jumps 1.4% at Close
data.description:
The Hang Seng surged 359 points or 1.4% to finish at 25,418 on Friday, snapping a three-day losing streak.

Optimism improved after Wall Street's S&P 500 hit a record high Thursday, with traders betting the Fed will cut rates this month amid further signs of a cooling U.S. labor market.

For the week, the index gained 1.4%, reversing the prior week’s loss, supported by a CNY 1 trillion liquidity injection from China’s central bank.

Still, upside was limited by the sharpest weekly drop in five months in mainland stocks as traders booked profits after a two-month rally and the end of China’s military parade.

All sectors participated in the rally, led by tech, consumers, and property.

Ubtech Robotics soared 4.4% on a CNY 250 million humanoid robot contract, while WuXi XDC Cayman climbed 3.6% after announcing a HKD 2.73 billion share sale.

Other top gainers included Horizon Robotics (9.5%), SMIC (6.0%), Hansoh Pharma (4.6%), and China Hongqiao (4.6%).

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0840 GMT - The European Central Bank is expected to leave interest rates on hold next Thursday and the market impact is likely to be limited, says Mediolanum's Niall Scanlon in a note. "We don't expect this meeting to be a market mover, as consensus points to a hold in rates from the ECB next week," the fixed income portfolio manager says. Economic data after the meeting will be more significant. Mediolanum expects data could weaken in the coming months as tariffs weighs on growth and German fiscal stimulus takes a while to feed through. Headline inflation is also forecast to be below target in 2026. The ECB could consider cutting rates again towards the end of 2025 or early in 2026, Scanlon says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0826 GMT - A much weaker-than-expected U.S. nonfarm payrolls report at 1230 GMT would have a bigger dollar impact than much stronger-than-forecast data, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. A major payrolls miss should prompt markets to price in three Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts by year-end, he says. The market is fully priced for two cuts, LSEG data show. A major upside surprise could be treated with "much more caution as markets might start to doubt the credibility of data under the new leadership at the Bureau of Labor Statistics." President Trump recently nominated conservative economist EJ Antoni to replace BLS head Erika McEntarfer. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 98.055. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0826 GMT - Long-dated yields are driven by growth and inflation expectations and by concerns about fiscal policy, says AXA Investment Managers' Chris Iggo in a note. Short-dated government bond yields on the other hand are influenced more by interest-rate expectations. This helps explain the recent trend for widening gaps between short-dated and long-dated yields as interest rates are reduced but longer-term economic worries persist. "The decline in global inflation rates since 2022 has allowed central banks to ease, a process that the market expects to continue," says the chair of AXA IM Investment Institute. Concerns that central banks might not meet inflation targets are helping to push long-dated yields higher, however, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0808 GMT - U.S. non-farm payrolls data due to be released at 1230 GMT are expected to show that jobs growth cooled in August but wasn't severely weak, RBC Capital Markets strategists say in a note. RBC expects the data to show a "cool but not frozen 64,000 jobs added in August," they say. However, any significant revisions to jobs figures for previous months could add to market volatility, RBC says. The consensus forecast in a WSJ poll predicts that the U.S. economy created 75,000 jobs in August, after 73,000 jobs were created in July. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0757 GMT - An upcoming confidence vote called by French Prime Minister Francois Bayrou over his budget plans is unlikely to have a significant impact on the euro, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. Bayrou is expected to lose the vote on Monday and a "realistic scenario" is that President Emmanuel Macron would then choose a new centrist or centre-right prime minister to deliver a watered-down fiscal consolidation package, he says. Political uncertainty will remain elevated but it probably won't be enough to trigger uncontrolled moves in French government bonds or the euro as a no-confidence outcome is largely priced in, he says. The euro rises 0.2% to $1.1672. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - Bitcoin rises to its highest in one week ahead of key U.S. jobs data at 1230 GMT as markets bet on the Federal Reserve resuming interest-rate cuts from the September 17 meeting. Weaker-than-expected ADP private payrolls data Thursday fuelled expectations for another soft nonfarm payroll report on Friday. This would strengthen the case for the Fed to cut rates later this month and likely beyond, potentially boosting risky assets including bitcoin. Bitcoin rises 2.1% to one-week high of $112,876, LSEG data show, extending gains after breaking back above $112,000. However, it remains well below the record high of $124,480 reached in mid-August, having recently fallen as traders took profits. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0747 GMT - The Indonesian rupiah could receive support from expectations for Fed rate cuts later this month, which is expected to renew broad-based weakness in the dollar, UOB's global economics and markets research team writes in a report. The rupiah has depreciated by 1.5% over the past two months, largely due to consecutive rate cuts by Bank Indonesia, UOB says. BI's dovish stance has weighed on the rupiah, making it one of the region's underperformers. The rupiah could also be weighed by negative sentiment in the near term from recent public protests, UOB says. BI's firm commitment to maintain currency stability is likely to help contain volatility. USD/IDR is little changed at 16,415, LSEG data shows. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0742 GMT - Gold prices edge higher in early trade after pausing from a record-breaking rally as traders locked in profits ahead of the release of key U.S. nonfarm payrolls data. Futures are up 0.1% to $3,610.40 a troy ounce and on track for a 2.6% weekly gain, after hitting a new high of $3,640.10/oz earlier this week. The precious metal's surge was largely driven by expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its next meeting, concerns about the U.S. central bank's independence following President Trump's move to fire Fed governor Lisa Cook, and persistent geopolitical risks. Now, "the biggest risk from a market perspective is an upside surprise to today's payrolls number," says Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB. "If rate cut bets get scaled back, then inflation fears could ease, which may hurt the world's most famous inflation hedge." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall along with their eurozone peers ahead of key U.S. non-farm payrolls data due at 1230 GMT. Weak jobs data could increase expectations for U.S. interest-rate cuts in the coming months, starting in September. They come after U.S. job openings data and ADP private payrolls data came in weaker than expected. "A reading around consensus would be a sign that the U.S. labor market is slowing down sharply," XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. Gilts shrug off U.K. retail sales data showing a solid monthly rise during July but a weaker-than-expected annual increase. Ten-year gilt yields fall 3 basis points to last trade at 4.707%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0719 GMT - Jefferies' bigger worry about France is about ratings rather than about politics. Fitch Ratings is due to review France on Sept. 12, and it already has a negative outlook. "If political uncertainty persists, we see a case for Fitch downgrading France," Jefferies' global economist Mohit Kumar says in a note. However, Moody's Ratings or S&P Global Ratings are unlikely to downgrade France, according to Jefferies. "Risk remains that if France loses the coveted AA level rating (currently AA-) from two or more rating agencies, it could lead to forced selling from some of the Asian accounts." For now, Jefferies remains bearish on French bonds and expects the 10-year French-German bond-yield spread to rise towards 90 basis points, from 77 basis points currently, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0702 GMT - Sterling edges higher after data showed U.K. retail sales rose slightly more than expected during July. Retail sales increased 0.6% in July compared to the previous month, above the 0.5% forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. The data come after Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey said earlier in the week that the timing of future interest-rate cuts is now more uncertain amid concerns about inflationary pressures. The market is pricing in a 36% chance of another rate cut by year-end, LSEG data show. Sterling rises to an intraday high of $1.3471 after the data, from $1.3460 beforehand. The euro falls to an intraday low of 0.8662, pounds from 0.8673 previously. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0637 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening slightly higher, with IG calling the OMXS30 up 0.3% at around 2615. The market is eagerly awaiting today's U.S. labor market data and how this will affect expectations of the Fed's interest-rate cuts, SEB's chief emerging markets strategist Erik Meyersson writes. SEB expects another month of employment growth of 75,000--in line with consensus--and unemployment to continue to rise slightly. "We think this will be enough for a 25 basis-point cut from the Fed in September." The money market is now pricing in at least two interest-rate cuts by the Fed before the end of the year, he says. OMXS30 closed at 2607.54, OMXN40 at 2293.02 and OBX at 1556.28. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Temenos Shares Dive After CEO Departure
data.description:
By Aimee Look

Temenos shares fell following the departure of its chief executive, Jean-Pierre Brulard, who held the role for 16 months.

Shares in the Swiss banking software company were trading 13% lower at 61.75 Swiss francs in European morning trade on Friday, wiping out the stock's gains since the start of the year.

Temenos said on Thursday its chief financial officer, Takis Spiliopoulos, would take over the CEO role on an interim basis, while the board looks for a permanent successor.

The decision raises questions about the company's long-term strategy, as it seems to stem from a difference in views between the board and Brulard, analysts at Jefferies wrote.

"Our interpretation is that the CEO wanted a bolder approach, while the board wanted less disruption to teams and more stability," the analysts said.

The company reiterated its short-term guidance, but volatility at the top might hinder investor sentiment, the analysts added.

Write to Aimee Look at aimee.look@wsj.com

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0434 ET - ASML Holding is expected to log stronger growth in the coming years, UBS analysts write in a research note as they upgrade the stock to buy from neutral. The Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment said in July that it could no longer guarantee growth in 2026 amid uncertainty from President Trump's tariffs. However, the European Union has since then reached a deal that set a 15% tariff ceiling for EU semiconductor exports to the U.S. The analysts say ASML will provide 2026 guidance at 3Q or 4Q results that will give investors a clearer picture. They expect ASML to log a compound annual growth rate of 20% in EPS between 2026 and the end of the decade. ASML shares trade 1.8% higher at 659.20 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0426 ET - A much weaker-than-expected U.S. nonfarm payrolls report at 1230 GMT would have a bigger dollar impact than much stronger-than-forecast data, ING's Francesco Pesole says in a note. A major payrolls miss should prompt markets to price in three Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts by year-end, he says. The market is fully priced for two cuts, LSEG data show. A major upside surprise could be treated with "much more caution as markets might start to doubt the credibility of data under the new leadership at the Bureau of Labor Statistics." President Trump recently nominated conservative economist EJ Antoni to replace BLS head Erika McEntarfer. The DXY dollar index falls 0.3% to 98.055. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0342 ET - Gold prices edge higher in early trade after pausing from a record-breaking rally as traders locked in profits ahead of the release of key U.S. nonfarm payrolls data. Futures are up 0.1% to $3,610.40 a troy ounce and on track for a 2.6% weekly gain, after hitting a new high of $3,640.10/oz earlier this week. The precious metal's surge was largely driven by expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its next meeting, concerns about the U.S. central bank's independence following President Trump's move to fire Fed governor Lisa Cook, and persistent geopolitical risks. Now, "the biggest risk from a market perspective is an upside surprise to today's payrolls number," says Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB. "If rate cut bets get scaled back, then inflation fears could ease, which may hurt the world's most famous inflation hedge." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0205 ET - A Trump executive order locks in a 15% base tariff rate for Japan, clearing some uncertainty but leaving key questions unanswered, says Stefan Angrick at Moody's Analytics. The order settles some doubts over car duties and cements Japan's pledge to buy more U.S. rice. But investment provisions remain murky, the economist says. Japan will funnel $550 billion into the U.S., though the composition is unclear. U.S. Commerce Secretary Lutnick insists it's real funding, while Tokyo has said the figure includes loans, guarantees, and equity via government-backed institutions, with direct equity accounting for just 1% to 2%, Angrick says. Economically, tariffs remain punishingly high. They are undercutting manufacturing and exports, and will trim at least half a percentage point from GDP. Politically, the deal offers scant relief for beleaguered PM Ishiba. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0155 ET - The U.S.'s cancellation of validated-end user status for Chinese units of some global chip makers could hurt the companies more than China, Jefferies analysts say in a research note. Neither Samsung nor SK Hynix makes superhigh-end memory products in their Chinese fabs, while TSMC likely produces legacy chips in China, the analysts say. These chip-making plants aren't important for China's AI efforts, they add. The analysts view the move as a U.S. response to Beijing's guidance to avoid using Nvidia's H20 chips. Jefferies thinks China might use its rare-earths leverage to get the U.S. to relax export controls on chip equipment. If Trump is willing to budge on this, it could be a big positive surprise to the market in 2026, it says.(sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2208 ET - Japan's top trade negotiator, Ryosei Akazawa, emphasized that its $550 billion investment deal with the U.S. will also be beneficial to Japan. "As a special partner to the U.S., Japan will cooperate to build supply chains for critical economic security sectors within the U.S. in a manner that also benefits Japan," Akazawa says. "It is natural that the intentions of the U.S. side--and even more so, President Trump's--will be strongly reflected." A memorandum of understanding signed by the two countries on Thursday assures Japan's participation in those discussions, he adds. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

2148 ET - Japan's top trade negotiator, Ryosei Akazawa, renewed his promise to take necessary measures to ease any negative effects of higher U.S. tariffs on the nation's corporate sector. President Trump on Thursday signed an order to lower tariffs on Japanese cars, which Akazawa says will likely take effect within a maximum of two weeks. While the 15% tariff will still hurt Japanese companies' profits, the clarity on the trade policy could also help stabilize the economy and support stock prices, Akazawa says. The Nikkei Stock Average is last up 0.8%. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

1118 ET - American Eagle's marketing campaigns with Sydney Sweeney and Travis Kelce generated 40 billion impressions and a record 700,000 new customers, but the company still has more work to do to improve its margins, Jefferies analysts say. Total revenue is still declining year-over-year and expenses are expected to surge in the third quarter due to elevated advertising spending. Meanwhile, tariffs are expected to cause a $60 million to $70 million hit to second-half margins. "While execution improved and brand heat is high, macro uncertainty, tariff drag, and flat FY comp guide (0%) suggest limited upside until visibility on margin recovery improves," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Chinese Robotics Company Geekplus Eyes New Listing as It Nears Profitability — Interview
data.description:
By Tracy Qu

Robotics company Geekplus Technology has big ambitions. It went public in Hong Kong a few months ago, and is already weighing another listing in China.

Geekplus, a known name in warehouse robotics with customers like Walmart, Adidas and UPS, is betting it can dominate the market as more companies use robots for industrial tasks.

An A-share listing in the next one to two years is under consideration, chief executive Zheng Yong said in a recent interview, though the process hasn't started yet.

The Beijing-based company, which has a market capitalization of over $3.8 billion, had a solid debut in Hong Kong. Shares gained 30% since it went public in July. But Zheng thinks the stock is undervalued.

As Geekplus expands, he hopes capital markets will better recognize the business's potential.

The Chinese company, founded by Zheng in 2015, faces competition as more robotics startups crop up and big players like Amazon advance their tech. It has yet to turn a profit but its latest earnings suggest that it is on the way.

Revenue in the first half of 2025 rose 31% compared with the same period a year earlier, while losses narrowed sharply--results the company said bring it close to a "profitability inflection point."

The goal is to maintain that momentum through the year, said Zheng, who has over 20 robotics-related patents.

Analysts at Deutsche Bank are bullish on the stock and began covering it with a buy rating.

The company is on track to hit full-year adjusted net profit for the first time, DB's Iris Zheng wrote in a note, citing management remarks. And it is well-placed to ride an upswing in the autonomous mobile robot industry, she added.

Warehouse automation boomed over 2019-2022 as the Covid-19 pandemic fueled demand for unmanned production and e-commerce sales. Momentum slowed over the past two years due to softer economic growth and rising interest rates but that is changing, DB said.

Part of Geekplus's appeal is its presence overseas, where it is more established than local peers, analysts say. Revenue from outside China accounted for nearly 80% of the group's total in the first half, signaling strong global demand.

Asked if rising trade barriers could hinder growth plans, Geekplus's CEO said warehouse robots are a relatively non-sensitive area.

Unlike advanced chips or AI, warehouse robots haven't been a flashpoint in the U.S.-China tech rivalry as the focus is on logistics, rather than other applications that could raise security concerns. Still, the supply for core and advanced components is vulnerable to an escalation in tensions.

As cross-border commerce gets more complex, Geekplus will invest more in localization, Zheng Yong said. "We are building our supply chain overseas."

Its next growth driver? So-called embodied intelligence.

Geekplus has set up a subsidiary dedicated to embodied intelligence, in which robots sense and learn on their own, instead of following pre-written programs. It also launched robot arms designed for warehousing, expanding from mobile robotics.

"Looking ahead, the development of embodied intelligence technologies...will increasingly support the company's revenue and financial performance," Geekplus's CEO said.

Write to Tracy Qu at tracy.qu@wsj.com

data.title: Hong Kong Foreign Reserves at 4-Month Low
data.description:
Foreign exchange reserves in Hong Kong declined to $421.6 billion in August 2025 from $425.4 billion in the previous month, marking the second consecutive monthly decline.

The reserves represented more than five times the currency in circulation, or about 37% of the Hong Kong dollar M3.

Including unsettled foreign exchange contracts, foreign currency reserve assets totaled $421.1 billion in August, compared with $424.5 billion in July 2025.

data.title: Codemill, Spherex and Embrace to Showcase Joint Compliance Workflow Solution at IBC 2025
data.description:
Umeå, Sweden, 5th of September 2025 - Codemill (Nasdaq: CDMIL), Spherex and Embrace announced they will debut a joint demo at IBC 2025 in Amsterdam, highlighting a groundbreaking compliance workflow solution that streamlines how media companies prepare content for global distribution.

Modern content supply chains are complex and resource-intensive, particularly when ensuring compliance with local regulations and cultural norms across markets. The joint solution integrates SpherexAI’s automated compliance checks with Codemill’s Accurate Video platform and Embrace’s orchestration technology to deliver faster, more efficient, and more accurate compliance workflows for film, television, advertising, and social video.

How It Works

The joint demo highlights a fully integrated compliance workflow designed to simplify and accelerate content preparation for global distribution:

  • Workflow Orchestration: Embrace streamlines content preparation and operational workflows by organizing media files, managing notifications, user jobs, and monitoring.
  • Automated Compliance Check: SpherexAI identifies objectionable content (including nudity, violence, and inappropriate language) and generates detailed metadata with contextual descriptions.
  • Metadata Review: Codemill’s Edit Suite ingests this metadata, enabling compliance teams to efficiently review flagged content and create lightweight compliance versions within an intuitive web-based UI.

By combining these technologies, global compliance checks can be completed in minutes rather than days, reducing costs while accelerating time-to-market.

Why It MattersPreparing content for international distribution has traditionally been slow and labor-intensive. SpherexAI’s advanced AI-driven compliance engine ensures unparalleled speed, depth, and accuracy. Integrated with Embrace’s orchestration and Codemill’s browser-based Edit Platform, the workflow provides content owners with multiple benefits, including:

  • Efficiency: Automation reduces lengthy manual review cycles.
  • Scalability: Seamlessly processes large content libraries.
  • Accuracy: Ensuring cultural sensitivity and regulatory compliance across 200+ countries and territories.
  • Cost-effectiveness: Cuts overhead by replacing manual compliance processes with AI-powered automation.

“Global media companies are under increasing pressure to deliver content faster while ensuring it complies with a complex web of cultural norms and regulatory standards,” said Teresa Phillips, CEO and co-founder of Spherex. “By integrating SpherexAI’s automated compliance intelligence with Codemill and Embrace’s products, we’re removing friction, improving accuracy, and empowering teams to prepare content for global distribution with greater speed and confidence.”

“With this demo we are showing how we can contribute to operational effectiveness for our customers,” said Codemill CEO Maria Hellström. “Integrating intelligent AI tools like SpherexAI, using Embrace, and being able to show the overview and the possibility to work with compliance in AV Edit, is a very strong message that we are here to support our customers in their need for efficiency.”

“Compliance shouldn’t slow great content. By orchestrating SpherexAI’s intelligence with

Codemill’s Edit Suite in Pulse-IT, we turn automated flags into actionable tasks, approvals, and renders, shrinking review cycles from days to minutes while giving operations full visibility and control. This is orchestration at work: connecting people, systems, and processes so our customers can work confidently in every market,” said Julien Gachot, CEO at Embrace. 

IBC attendees can see a demo of the joint compliance workflow solution at the Codemill and Embrace Stand 6.C11.

Attendees can also see a demo of SpherexAI at the Spherex Stand 5.A21.

About Codemill 

Codemill is a Swedish technology company that develops software and solutions for leading video and media companies globally. Codemill's products Accurate Player SDK, Accurate.Video, and Cantemo are used by the world's largest players in broadcast, VOD/OOT, Content Supply Chain platforms, and tools to streamline and automate media production. Customers include Paramount Global, WarnerBros Discovery, NBC Universal, BBC, ITV, ProSiebenSat.1 Media, The Telegraph, and several other leading brands.

The company is listed on Nasdaq First North Growth Market.

Certified Adviser is DNB Carnegie Investment Bank AB.

About Spherex

Spherex is a global technology company transforming how media and entertainment enterprises create, adapt, and deliver film and television to audiences worldwide through expert-centered AI and machine learning. With unmatched expertise in culture and regulatory compliance, Spherex works with the world's largest media companies, movie studios, networks, distributors, and streamers to build larger audiences, speed up content discovery, drive more video views, and generate higher revenue. Learn more at www.spherex.com.

About Embrace 

Since 2015, Embrace has been transforming content creation at scale by connecting people, systems and processes. The company develops advanced automation, orchestration and collaboration solutions for the Media & Entertainment industry and global brands. Embrace aims to unleash creativity and improve performance around video and graphics supply chains.

Our products are heavily used 24/7 by leading media groups such as AMC Networks, Arte Studio, BCE, Be tv, CANAL+, Disney-ABC News, Euronews, Eurosport, Hearst Networks EMEA, Madison Square Garden Networks, Mediawan Thematics, M6, Mercedes-AMG, Orange, Red Bee Media, RTL Group, ProSiebenSat1, Sinclair, TF1, TV5MONDE, Warner Bros. Discovery.

Visit www.embrace.fr for more information.

Media Contacts: 

Johan Bergström, Codemill, johan.bergstrom@codemill.se

Tracy Akselrud, Spherex, tracy.akselrud@spherex.com

data.title: China slaps preliminary duties on EU pork imports
data.description:
China on Friday placed preliminary anti-dumping duties as high as 62.4% on certain pork imports from the European Union.

The preliminary ruling found evidence of dumping that damaged the domestic industry and approved duties ranging from 15.6% to 62.4% depending on the producer that will start on September 10th, according to a release from the Ministry of Commerce on Friday.

The finding is preliminary and could be changed or reversed when the investigation ends in December.

Launched in June last year, the investigation is widely seen as retaliation for EU tariffs on Chinese electric vehicle exports and has hit over $2 billion in pork exports, concentrated in major producers such as Spain, the Netherlands, and Denmark.

data.title: Foxconn: Expect Cloud, Networking Product Shipments to Rise Significantly in 3Q
Global Markets Rise Amid Increasing Fed Rate-Cut Expectations
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

Markets looked set to close out the week on a more positive note than they started it. Equities were mostly in the green early on Friday and bond yields continued to fall. The Federal Reserve is increasingly expected to cut interest rates at its next meeting and U.S. nonfarm payroll data, due at 0830 ET, are expected to cement those expectations further; the U.S. likely added 75,000 jobs in August compared with July's 73,000, according to a Wall Street Journal poll of economists, while the unemployment rate is expected to edge up to 4.3% from 4.2% a month earlier.

  • U.S. stock futures were up modestly after the S&P 500 hit a new record in the previous session.
  • Asian indexes closed higher with China recouping most of its declines from Thursday. Japan's Nikkei closed 1.0% higher, supported by financial and automobile stocks after the U.S. lowered tariffs on Japanese automobiles and auto parts from 25% to 15%. South Korea's Kospi saw a bit of profit-taking from past session gains, but managed to close 0.1% higher.
  • In Europe, the pan-European Stoxx Europe 600 eked out a 0.1% increase in morning trading reflecting small gains across major national indexes. Still, France's CAC 40 declined 0.2%; deteriorating public finances in France are causing political fragmentation and Prime Minister Francois Bayrou isn't expected to survive a confidence vote due Sept. 8. After that, Fitch Ratings is due to review France on Sept. 12.
  • The U.S. dollar eased ahead of nonfarm payrolls; the DXY dollar index against a basket of major currencies was last down 0.2% to 98.113.
  • U.S. Treasury yields edged lower, extending the previous day's move. The two-year Treasury yield was last down 0.9 basis point to 3.582%; the 10-year yield fell 2.1 basis points to 4.154% and the 30-year yield dropped 3 basis points to 3.842%, according to Tradeweb.
  • Eurozone government bond yields tracked U.S. Treasury yields lower with eurozone 10-year bond yields dropping by up to 4 basis points. Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall along with their eurozone peers and shrugging off U.K. retail sales data showing a solid monthly rise during July but a weaker-than-expected annual increase. The 10-year gilt yield was down 3 basis points to last trade at 4.707%, according to Tradeweb data. In Japan, the 30-year Japanese Government Bond yield was down 3 basis points to 3.232%, according to LSEG.
  • In commodities, oil prices were set for a weekly loss of more than 1% for the first time in three weeks amid concerns over OPEC+ supply and an unexpected build in U.S. crude stockpiles. In early European trade, Brent crude was down 0.5% to $66.67 a barrel. OPEC+'s online meeting on Sunday will be closely watched by traders, as further production increases would exacerbate concerns over a looming supply glut.
  • Gold prices edged higher after pausing from a record-breaking rally as traders locked in profits.

data.title: August's U.K. House Price Rise Points to a Recovery — Market Talk
data.description: The latest data from the Halifax House Price Index points to a second-half recovery as mortgage approvals are back to prepandemic levels, Goodbody analyst Max Mulcahy says in a note. Still, although risks remain skewed to the downside--with weak public finances and the threat of tax rises and spending cuts in the U.K.'s autumn budget looming--Mulcahy expects a recovery and modest house price inflation in 2025. "The confirmation of Nov. 26th as Budget Day makes it unusually late and means we now have 12 weeks of leaks and elevated uncertainty which is likely to temper consumer-decision making over the coming months," Mulcahy says. Shares in Crest Nicholson are up 2.2%, followed by Barratt Redrow and Berkeley, both up 1.4%. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Circio Holding ASA - Conversion of Convertible Bonds and funding requested under Additional Financing Commitment from Atlas
data.description:
Reference is made to Circio Holding ASA's (the "Company") outstanding convertible bonds as issued to Atlas Special Opportunities, LLC ("Atlas") under the Investment Agreement entered into between Atlas and the Company in March 2023.

Atlas has, by a notice of conversion, requested conversion of convertible bonds with a nominal value of NOK 2,000,000. Pursuant to the bond terms, the conversion price is NOK 0.5771. As the conversion price cannot be set lower than the nominal value of the share, the conversion price for the conversion is set to NOK 0.6 and the NOK 2,000,000 convertible bonds are convertible into 3,333,333 new shares in the Company.

The Company's share capital will accordingly be increased by NOK 1,999,999.80 by the issuance of 3,333,333 new shares in the Company at a conversion price of NOK 0.6, upon completion of the conversion by registration of the share capital increase in the Norwegian Register of Business Enterprises (Nw.: Foretaksregisteret). Following the conversion, the Company's total share capital will be NOK 65,305,046.40 divided into 108,841,744 shares, each with a nominal value of NOK 0.60. The difference between NOK 0.6 and NOK 0.5771 a value of approximately NOK 76,000, will be settled separately with Atlas according to the Investment Agreement and applicable laws and regulations.

Furthermore, reference is made to the stock exchange announcement by the Company on 4 August 2025 regarding the extension of the financing commitment by Atlas, enabling funding flexibility to the end of the fourth quarter 2025 (the "Additional Financing Commitment"). The Company has by a notice of exercise under the Agreement and the Additional Financing Commitment requested Atlas to subscribe and pay for one tranche of convertible bonds, consisting of 8 convertible bonds with an aggregate principal amount equal to NOK 4,000,000. Atlas will be allocated these bonds under the terms and conditions of the Investment Agreement.
